summary:
The Turkish War of Independence (1919–1923) was the military and political campaign led by Mustafa Kemal that resulted in the abolition of the Ottoman sultanate and the establishment of the Republic of Turkey. Following the Ottoman defeat in [[event:110|World War I]] and the subsequent Allied occupation of Constantinople and partition of Ottoman territories under the Treaty of Sèvres (1920), nationalist forces organized in Anatolia under Mustafa Kemal's leadership rejected the terms imposed by the Allied powers and the acquiescent Ottoman government.

The war involved multiple fronts. Greek forces, who had landed at Smyrna (Izmir) in 1919 under Allied authorization, advanced deep into western Anatolia. Mustafa Kemal's forces, organized as the Grand National Assembly's army, fought the Greeks, Armenian forces in the east, French forces in the south, and resisted the nominal Ottoman government backed by British forces. The Greek advance was checked and then decisively reversed in the Battle of the Sakarya in 1921 and the subsequent Great Offensive of August–September 1922, which drove Greek forces to the sea at Smyrna in catastrophic circumstances for the Greek population of the city.

The war's conclusion produced a major demographic transformation. The Treaty of Lausanne (1923) established the boundaries of the new Turkish state and sanctioned the compulsory exchange of populations between Greece and Turkey — approximately 1.5 million Greek Orthodox Christians were transferred from Turkey to Greece, and about 500,000 Muslims moved from Greece to Turkey — one of the largest forced population exchanges in history.

Mustafa Kemal, who adopted the surname Atatürk (Father of the Turks), subsequently used his political authority to transform Turkey into a secular republic, abolishing the caliphate in 1924 and implementing sweeping modernizing reforms in law, language, dress, and governance.
